name: FB News Feed
overview:
Facebook News Feed aggregates posts from a user's friends and followed pages, ranks them by relevance,
and displays them in a personalized feed. The system must handle 500M daily active users, each with up to
5,000 friends, and serve feed reads with <200ms latency. The ranking algorithm considers recency,
engagement signals, and user preferences. The system must handle celebrity accounts (millions of followers)
differently from regular users to avoid fan-out storms.

inScope: Generate personalized feed for each user
Include posts from friends, followed pages, and groups

items: Support 500M DAU; each user may have up to 5,000 friends
99.99% availability — feed downtime is immediately visible to hundreds of millions of users
Eventual consistency: slight feed staleness (seconds) is acceptable over unavailability
Fan-out must handle celebrity accounts (millions of followers) without causing write storms
Horizontally scalable feed cache; handle 10x traffic spikes during breaking news events

entities.name: Post
entities.schema:
{
  post_id: UUID,
  author_id: string,
  content: string,
  media_urls: [string],
  created_at: timestamp,
  like_count: int,
  comment_count: int,
  share_count: int
}

entities.name: FeedItem

endpoints.response:
{ posts: [{ post_id, author, content, media, like_count, ... }],
    next_cursor, has_more }

approaches.title: Pull (Fan-out on Read)
approaches.description: When a user requests their feed, query all their friends' posts, merge, sort by score, and return top N. For a user with 5,000 friends, this requires 5,000 DB queries or a massive JOIN. At 500M DAU, this is computationally prohibitive.
approaches.tradeoff: Too slow for users with many friends; high read amplification.

approaches.title: Push (Fan-out on Write)
approaches.description: When a user posts, immediately write the post to the feed of all their followers. Fast reads (pre-computed feed). But for a celebrity with 10M followers, one post triggers 10M writes — a fan-out storm that can take minutes.
approaches.tradeoff: Fan-out storm for celebrity accounts; wasted writes for inactive users.

approaches.title: Hybrid: Push for Regular Users, Pull for Celebrities
approaches.description: Regular users (<10k followers): fan-out on write. Post is pushed to all followers' feed caches immediately. Celebrity users (>10k followers): fan-out on read. Celebrity posts are fetched and merged at read time. Feed cache stores pre-ranked post_ids per user. At read time, post details are fetched from a post cache (Redis). Celebrity posts are injected during the merge step.
approaches.tradeoff: More complex logic; celebrity threshold requires tuning; merge step adds latency.

approaches.title: Chronological Sort
approaches.description: Sort posts by created_at descending. Simple and predictable. Users miss important posts from friends who post infrequently. Engagement signals are ignored.
approaches.tradeoff: Poor user experience; high-volume posters dominate the feed.

approaches.title: Simple Engagement Score
approaches.description: Score = recency_weight × (1/age_hours) + engagement_weight × (likes + comments × 2 + shares × 3). Better than chronological but doesn't personalize per user. A post with 1000 likes is ranked the same for all users regardless of their relationship with the author.
approaches.tradeoff: No personalization; engagement farming can game the score.

approaches.title: ML Ranking with Feature Store
approaches.description: A lightweight ML model (gradient boosted trees) scores each candidate post using features: author_affinity (how often user interacts with author), post_engagement_rate, content_type_preference, recency. Features are pre-computed and stored in a Feature Store (Redis). The ranking model runs in <10ms per post. Model is retrained daily on engagement data. A/B testing framework evaluates ranking changes.
approaches.tradeoff: ML model adds complexity; feature store requires maintenance; model can amplify biases.

components.name: Post Service
components.responsibility: Accepts new posts; triggers fan-out for regular users; writes to Post DB

components.name: Fan-out Service
components.responsibility: Consumes post events from Kafka; writes post_id to followers' feed caches in Redis

components.name: Feed Service
components.responsibility: Serves feed requests; reads from Redis feed cache; merges celebrity posts; ranks with ML model

components.name: Redis Feed Cache
components.responsibility: Stores pre-ranked post_id lists per user (max 1000 items); TTL = 7 days

components.name: Post Cache (Redis)
components.responsibility: Caches post details (content, media, counts) for fast hydration

components.name: Feature Store (Redis)
components.responsibility: Stores pre-computed ranking features per (user, post) pair

components.name: Ranking Service
components.responsibility: Applies ML model to score candidate posts; returns ranked list

components.name: Post DB (MySQL)
components.responsibility: Durable store for all posts; sharded by author_id

components.name: Social Graph DB (TAO)
components.responsibility: Stores friend/follow relationships; used for fan-out target list

questions.title: 2. Feed Cache Eviction
questions.content:
The feed cache stores up to 1000 post_ids per user. When the cache is full, the oldest (lowest-scored) post_ids
are evicted. For inactive users (no login in 7 days), the feed cache is not maintained — their feed is generated
on-demand at login time by querying the Post DB directly. This avoids wasting Redis memory on inactive users.

questions.title: 3. Handling Deleted Posts
questions.content:
When a post is deleted, it must be removed from all feed caches. For regular users, the fan-out service sends
delete events to all followers' caches. For celebrity posts (not in feed caches), the Feed Service checks post
status at read time and filters deleted posts. A post_id blocklist in Redis provides O(1) lookup for deleted posts
during feed hydration.

questions.title: 4. Pagination with Cursors
questions.content:
Feed pagination uses cursor-based pagination (not offset-based) to handle the dynamic nature of feeds. The
cursor encodes the last seen post_id and score. On the next request, the Feed Service returns posts with score
< cursor_score (or same score but post_id < cursor_post_id). This ensures consistent pagination even as new

posts are inserted at the top of the feed.

questions.title: 5. Real-Time Feed Updates
questions.content:
When a friend posts, the user's feed should update without a page refresh. The Feed Service maintains a
WebSocket connection per active user. When a new post is fan-out to a user's feed cache, a notification is
pushed via WebSocket. The client then fetches the new post and prepends it to the displayed feed. This gives
the 'X new posts' banner seen on Facebook.

design.diagram:
Facebook News Feed — System Design
Post · Fan-out · Rank · Serve
User
browser/app
Post Svc
Feed Svc
Kafka
post events
Fan-out Svc
regular users
Feed Service
merge + rank
Redis Feed
post_id lists
Post Cache
post details
Ranking Svc
ML model
Social Graph
TAO / friends
POST /posts
GET /feed
publish
consume
get followers
write feed cache
read feed
read post_ids
hydrate posts
rank candidates
keyFlows: ① User posts → Post Svc → Kafka → Fan-out Svc
② Fan-out: get followers from Social Graph → write post_id to Redis Feed Cache
③ Celebrity posts: NOT fanned out; merged at read time by Feed Service
④ GET /feed → Feed Svc → Redis Feed Cache → hydrate from Post Cache
⑤ Ranking Svc applies ML model (author affinity + engagement + recency)

whatToStudyFurther.content:
Expert Note: Study Facebook's TAO (The Associations and Objects) system: how it stores the social graph
as objects and associations, and how it handles the massive read load of social graph queries.
Expert Note: Learn about the fan-out-on-write vs. fan-out-on-read trade-off in depth: the exact thresholds
used by Twitter (celebrity threshold ~10k followers) and the hybrid approach.
Expert Note: Understand gradient boosted trees (XGBoost/LightGBM) for ranking: how they handle sparse
features, the role of feature importance, and how to serve them at low latency.
Expert Note: Study Redis cluster architecture: how hash slots are distributed, how resharding works, and
how to handle hot keys in a feed cache scenario.
Expert Note: Learn about the EdgeRank algorithm (Facebook's original ranking) and how modern
ML-based ranking systems differ from it in terms of feature engineering and model complexity.

Facebook News Feed
